skill.name: slack-gif-creator
skill.description: Create and validate animated GIFs for Slack messages and custom emoji using Python animation templates, drawing helpers, and size optimization utilities.
skill.summary: Build Slack-ready GIFs and animated emoji with validation and optimization helpers.
skill.author: ComposioHQ

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many high-risk indicators, but review shows they are false positives from Markdown examples, license text, animation terminology, and local drawing code. The skill contains executable Python utilities that read and write local GIF files and asks users to install dependencies, so it should be published with a low-risk local execution notice. No prompt-injection attempt, active network call, credential access, shell execution, or malicious intent was found.

low_findings.title: Expected Local File Read and Write Access
low_findings.description: The toolkit writes GIF files, reads GIF metadata, checks file sizes, and loads local fonts. This is expected for GIF creation, but users should choose output paths carefully and avoid running it on untrusted image files.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Direct Path, stat, Image.open, imageio.imwrite, and local font loading calls are present. Their purpose is local GIF generation and validation, with no evidence of unauthorized file traversal or data exfiltration.

low_findings.title: Template Modules Modify Python Import Path
low_findings.description: Template modules append the skill parent directory to sys.path so examples can import core helpers. This is a common local script pattern, but it can import unintended modules if the directory is tampered with.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The import path change is clearly present and uses the file parent directory, which limits the scope. Risk depends on local directory integrity rather than malicious behavior in the reviewed code.

low_findings.title: Markdown Code Blocks Are Not Automatic Execution
low_findings.description: The external command and dynamic import detections in SKILL.md are mostly fenced Python examples and a dependency installation command. They require deliberate user execution and are not run by the skill itself, but users should review generated code before running it.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The evidence is contained in Markdown fences and example snippets, not in an auto-run installer or hidden hook. A real user-execution risk remains because the documentation asks users to run Python examples and pip install dependencies.

low_findings.title: Static Malware Indicators Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The weak cryptography, C2 keyword, and reconnaissance detections match ordinary words, license text, animation variable names, and visual design comments. I found no cryptographic implementation, command-and-control behavior, or reconnaissance logic in these contexts.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The inspected lines are comments, documentation, license wording, or animation math. No matching line performs encryption, networking, host enumeration, or remote control behavior.

low_findings.title: No Active Network Behavior Found
low_findings.description: The only hardcoded URLs found in reviewed evidence are Apache License references. They are static text and are not fetched or called by the skill.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs are inside a standard license file. A targeted search found no requests, urllib, socket, webhook, curl, or wget usage in the reviewed skill files.

content.user_title: Create Slack GIFs and Emoji Animations
content.value_statement: Slack GIFs often fail strict size and dimension limits. This skill provides animation templates, drawing helpers, and validation tools for Slack-ready GIFs.
actual_capabilities: Creates GIF frames with templates for shake, bounce, move, spin, fade, zoom, and related effects.
Builds Slack message GIFs and custom emoji GIFs with configurable dimensions, frame counts, and colors.
Optimizes GIF output by reducing colors, resizing emoji GIFs, and removing near-duplicate frames.
Validates GIF file size, dimensions, frame count, duration, and Slack readiness.
Draws text, emoji, shapes, gradients, particles, flashes, and motion effects with reusable helpers.
limitations: Requires local Python dependencies, including Pillow, imageio, and numpy.
Does not upload GIFs to Slack or call the Slack API.
Slack emoji GIFs may need several optimization passes to stay under 64 KB.
Emoji and font rendering can vary by operating system and installed fonts.

use_cases.title: Design Branded Reactions
use_cases.description: Create lightweight animated reactions that match a team event, campaign, or product launch.

use_cases.title: Validate Custom Emoji
use_cases.description: Check animated emoji dimensions and file size before adding them to a Slack workspace.

use_cases.title: Prototype Team Announcements
use_cases.description: Generate short animated GIFs for launch posts, incident updates, celebrations, or internal rituals.

prompt_templates.title: Simple Emoji Animation
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner request for a small Slack emoji GIF.
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a 128 by 128 Slack emoji GIF of a party popper bouncing twice. Keep it simple and validate the final size.

prompt_templates.title: Message Reaction GIF
prompt_templates.scenario: Intermediate request for a Slack message GIF.
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a Slack message GIF that says Great work with confetti, a clean background, and a loop under two megabytes.

prompt_templates.title: Custom Motion Sequence
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced request with multiple animation phases.
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a three phase Slack GIF where a rocket enters, shakes on launch, and leaves a sparkle trail. Optimize colors after export.

prompt_templates.title: Optimize Existing Concept
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced request focused on constraints and iteration.
prompt_templates.prompt: Build a Slack emoji GIF from this concept: a warning sign pulses, flashes once, and settles. Reduce frames until it passes the 64 KB limit.

output_examples.input: A small animated emoji of a smiling face shaking with excitement.
output_examples.output: A 128 by 128 looping emoji GIF with a short shake motion, simple colors, and Slack size validation feedback.

output_examples.input: A celebration GIF for a team launch announcement.
output_examples.output: A square Slack message GIF with animated confetti, bold readable text, optimized colors, and a file size report.

output_examples.input: A bouncing ball GIF that must stay lightweight.
output_examples.output: A short looping GIF with eased bounce motion, reduced duplicate frames, and optimization suggestions if the file is too large.

best_practices: Start with fewer frames and colors, then increase detail only if the file stays within Slack limits.
Use 128 by 128 dimensions for emoji GIFs and validate size after every export.
Keep text short, high contrast, and centered so it remains readable at Slack display sizes.
anti_patterns: Do not use long animation timelines for Slack emoji, because the 64 KB limit is strict.
Do not rely on gradients or many colors when the GIF must be very small.
Do not run generated Python code on untrusted images or write outputs to sensitive directories.

faq.question: Can this skill upload GIFs directly to Slack?
faq.answer: No. It creates and validates local GIF files, but it does not call the Slack API.

faq.question: What dependencies are required?
faq.answer: The documented dependencies are Pillow, imageio, and numpy. Install them only in a trusted local environment.

faq.question: Can it make Slack custom emoji?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes emoji-focused settings for 128 by 128 dimensions, fewer frames, and smaller color palettes.

faq.question: Why is the 64 KB Slack emoji limit difficult?
faq.answer: Animated emoji need many frames, but Slack has a very small file limit. Simple designs usually compress best.

faq.question: Does it use network access?
faq.answer: No active network behavior was found in the reviewed skill files. License URLs are static text only.

faq.question: Is generated code safe to run?
faq.answer: Review the output path and source images first. The toolkit writes local files and reads local GIF metadata.
